era_context: The Byzantine-Sasanid War (602–628 CE) reaches a critical phase with Sasanid forces advancing deep into Byzantine territory. In Arabia, Muhammad's prophetic mission is in its early years (c. 610–615 CE). The Sui Dynasty in China is consolidating control but facing internal strains. Western Europe remains fragmented under Merovingian Frankish rule. This is a period of religious ferment across multiple civilizations: Christianity is dominant in the Mediterranean and spreading in Northern Europe; Zoroastrianism remains the state religion of Sasanid Persia; Buddhism is flourishing in China and spreading across Asia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Southeast Asia (reliant on later Chinese records and sparse epigraphy)
Americas (no written records; archaeological dating uncertain)

events.title: Byzantine-Sasanid War: Sasanid forces capture Jerusalem
events.description: Sasanid Persian forces under General Shahrbaraz capture the Byzantine city of Jerusalem after a siege. The city, one of the holiest sites in Christendom, fell to Sasanid control. According to Byzantine and Armenian sources, the capture was accompanied by significant destruction and the removal of the True Cross relic to Ctesiphon (the Sasanid capital). This event was a traumatic blow to Byzantine Christendom and symbolized the nadir of Byzantine power in the eastern Mediterranean during the Byzantine-Sasanid War.

sources.name: Antiochus Strategos, Account of the Sack of Jerusalem

events.certainty_note: The capture of Jerusalem is attested in multiple Byzantine, Armenian, and Syriac sources, though all were written after the events (50–200 years later). The date 614 CE is the scholarly consensus, though some sources suggest 613 CE. The removal of the True Cross is mentioned in multiple sources but the details vary.
cross_references: 628-001 (End of Byzantine-Sasanid War and recovery of True Cross)

events.title: Sui Dynasty: Yangdi's military campaigns and administrative consolidation
events.description: Emperor Yangdi continues his reign with military campaigns and administrative reforms aimed at consolidating Sui control over the reunified Chinese empire. The period 614–615 CE saw Yangdi's focus on military expeditions against the Göktürks in the north and continued infrastructure development. However, these campaigns were increasingly costly and unpopular, contributing to the growing discontent that would eventually lead to the Sui Dynasty's collapse.

sources.name: Sui Shu (History of the Sui Dynasty), compiled 636 CE
Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government), compiled 11th century CE

events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Yangdi's reign is well-documented in Chinese sources, but specific events in 614 CE are not precisely dated. The year falls within the documented period of his military campaigns and administrative activities.

events.title: Early Islamic period: Growing opposition to Muhammad in Mecca
events.description: Muhammad's preaching in Mecca continues to face opposition from the Quraysh merchant elite. By this period (approximately 4–5 years into his mission), his followers remained a small minority, and persecution of early Muslims intensified. The traditional Islamic sources describe this as a period of hardship for the Muslim community, with some followers emigrating to Abyssinia (Ethiopia) to escape persecution.

sources.name: Ibn Ishaq, Sirat Rasul Allah (Life of the Prophet, compiled 8th century CE)
Al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings (9th century CE)

events.certainty_note: The chronology of early Islamic events relies entirely on Islamic tradition and later biographical compilations. No contemporary non-Islamic sources document these events. The traditional Islamic chronology places this period in the early Meccan phase of Muhammad's mission, but the exact dating is uncertain.

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Jerusalem's capture is sometimes placed in 613 CE by certain scholars, though 614 CE is the more common scholarly consensus. The details of the capture and the fate of the True Cross vary significantly across sources. Some modern scholars have questioned whether the True Cross was actually removed to Ctesiphon or whether this is a later legendary embellishment. Regarding early Islam, no contemporary non-Islamic sources mention Muhammad or early Islamic events in 614 CE. The Islamic biographical tradition, compiled 150+ years later, is the sole source for these events.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 614 CE is dominated by later compilations and chronicles written 50–300 years after the events. Byzantine sources are filtered through Christian theological concerns and were composed in the 8th–9th centuries. Armenian sources (Sebeos) provide an alternative perspective but are also later compilations. Islamic sources derive entirely from oral tradition compiled in the 8th century CE. Chinese sources reflect Tang Dynasty perspectives on the Sui Dynasty. The record is heavily biased toward elite political and military events; the experiences of ordinary people, women, and non-elite populations are largely absent. Archaeological evidence for the destruction of Jerusalem in 614 CE is limited.

graph_edges.note: Part of the broader Sasanid offensive in the Levant (614–615 CE)
The capture of Jerusalem was reversed when Heraclius defeated the Sasanids in 628 CE
Yangdi's costly military campaigns contributed to Sui instability and collapse
